title: Cowork (Microsoft Copilot Cowork)
summary: Anthropic-powered agentic Copilot built into M365. P3 strategy: become a Cowork plugin via Graph Connector + Skills, not compete with it.
content:
Cowork (full name: Microsoft Copilot Cowork) is an agentic Copilot experience inside M365 powered by Anthropic Claude models (Opus 4.7 since May 2026). It is in Frontier preview as of March 2026 and requires M365 Copilot Premium + Frontier enrollment + Anthropic subprocessor approval.

P3 strategic stance: do not build a competing chat front-end. Instead, push P3 enrichment content into Cowork's grounding layer via this Graph Connector, expose P3 capabilities as Skills/Plugins, and let Cowork handle the conversation UX.

Surface area for P3 content in Cowork: ambient grounding (this connector), Plugins (declarative agent manifests), Skills (Anthropic Skills Server at p3ai.ai/skills), Agent 365 channel (M365Extensions, used by p3-iq-cea).
author: P3 IQ Practice

title: Translytical Task Flows
summary: Power BI feature blending transactional writeback with analytical reads. P3 ships translytical via Python UDFs published headlessly through Playwright (zero-UI pipeline).
content:
Translytical Task Flows is a Power BI feature that allows users to trigger transactional operations (writes, updates, workflow kicks) directly from a report, while still benefiting from analytical model performance.

P3 zero-UI pipeline: author UDF in Python -> publish via REST -> headless Playwright to inject button into report -> users click button in PBI service. This bypasses the Power BI UI entirely for the publish step, enabling full IaC for translytical UDFs.

Reference implementation: see p3-powerbi-mcp tools publish_udf and inject_button. Production examples: forecast adjustments in Sunrise Sales Review, ARR scenario writeback in Aderant.
author: P3 IQ Practice

title: DirectLake (Microsoft Fabric)
summary: Storage mode in Fabric that reads parquet files directly from OneLake, combining Import-mode performance with DirectQuery freshness. P3's default for new Fabric workloads.
content:
DirectLake is a storage mode unique to Microsoft Fabric. It loads parquet files from OneLake into the in-memory VertiPaq engine on-demand, achieving Import-mode query speed without a refresh schedule and without the latency of DirectQuery.

P3 stance: DirectLake is the default for new Fabric semantic models on lakehouse data. Fall back to Import mode only when complex transformations require Power Query, or when the source isn't OneLake-resident.

Gotchas: DirectLake falls back to DirectQuery for queries that exceed memory limits (the 'fallback to DirectQuery' behavior). Composite models with DirectLake + Import are supported but require careful relationship design. Calculation groups and field parameters work; calculated columns persist (computed at refresh-equivalent moments).
author: P3 IQ Practice

title: Archie (P3 Design Thinking Coach)
summary: P3's flagship internal AI coach: a 13-phase design thinking simulation centered on Marcus, a wheelchair-using gourmet cook, with a Mentor voice running Socratic escalation.
content:
Archie is P3's design thinking coaching agent, built on Anthropic Skills + LibreChat. It simulates a discovery interview with Marcus (a fictitious client who uses a wheelchair and is a gourmet cook) and a Mentor voice that coaches the user through 13 phases of design thinking.

Architecture: Archie skill at archie-discovery-coach, MCP server at p3-archie-mcp (port 8082, FastMCP SSE), state persisted in state.json per session. ACL: public on staging, group-gated on production via archie_access.json in a named Docker volume.

Triggers: 'archie', 'discovery coach', 'design thinking exercise', 'Marcus exercise', 'kitchen remodel exercise'.
author: P3 IQ Practice

title: Bridge Mode (P3 MCP Auth Pattern)
summary: P3's preferred MCP-to-MCP auth pattern: shared secret + forwarded user identity headers. Three headers: X-Bridge-Token, X-Microsoft-Token, X-User-Email.
content:
Bridge Mode is P3's standard auth pattern for inter-MCP trust within the platform. The calling service (e.g., LibreChat or p3-iq-cea) presents three headers to the downstream MCP:

  • X-Bridge-Token: shared secret, validates the caller is a trusted P3 service.
  • X-Microsoft-Token: the user's delegated AAD access token for the downstream API audience (Graph, Search, etc.). Forwarded verbatim for ACL-trimmed retrieval.
  • X-User-Email: convenience header for logging and tenant-isolation checks.

Why bridge mode beats JWT/OBO: simpler operationally; no token-exchange chain inside MCP; preserves user identity for audit; works with the streamable-HTTP transport without per-call OAuth dance.

Critical: never use SSE transport with bridge mode + per-user OBO -- the SSE GET task context loses the per-POST resolved headers. Always streamable-HTTP for delegated-token MCPs.
author: P3 IQ Practice
